lessons.title: 500 Internal Server Error — System Failure
lessons.context: A 500 error means the server crashed or encountered an unhandled exception. This is almost always a backend bug or infrastructure issue — not a test code problem. Attempting to heal test code in response to a 500 wastes time and obscures the real issue.

problem.cause: Backend system failure — unhandled exception, database error, or infrastructure issue

antipattern.description: Trying to fix test code when the server returns 500
antipattern.why_bad: 500 is a server-side problem — no amount of test code changes will fix it
code: // BAD — retrying or modifying test code for 500
// This is an infrastructure issue, not a test code issue

solution.description: Skip the test and report the 500 to the dev team. Do not attempt to heal test code for server errors.
code: // Skipped: POST /users returns 500 — backend issue, reported to dev team

lessons.ai_instruction: When a test fails with 500, do NOT attempt to heal the test code. Mark the test as skipped with a comment explaining it is a server-side issue.

lessons.title: Request Timeout — Progressive Debugging Strategy
lessons.context: Timeouts can have many root causes: slow database queries, network issues, server overload, or a too-short timeout setting. Use a progressive approach to find and fix the root cause rather than permanently increasing the timeout.

problem.cause: API slow response, server not responding, network issue, or timeout setting too low

antipattern.description: Permanently increasing timeout without investigating root cause
antipattern.why_bad: Masks slow queries or infrastructure problems — tests become slow and the real bug goes unfixed
code: // BAD — permanent timeout increase without investigation
test.setTimeout(120000); // Just made it longer, didn't fix anything

solution.description: Use progressive debugging: quick fix first to unblock, then investigate root cause, then fix permanently.

steps.name: Root Cause Analysis
checklist: 1. Check Network Log: Does request reach server?
2. Check API Endpoint: Is URL correct?
3. Check Server Status: Is API server running normally?
4. Check Request Payload: Is sent data complete?
5. Check Database: Is query slow or deadlocked?

steps.name: Permanent Fix
solutions: API slow → Optimize query, add index, or cache
Network slow → Check VPN, proxy, firewall
Payload large → Reduce size or use pagination
Server overload → Scale resources or load balance

lessons.ai_instruction: When a test fails with timeout, apply Round 1 to unblock, then add a TODO comment to investigate root cause. Never permanently increase timeout without documenting why.

lessons.title: CORS Error — Use APIRequestContext Instead of page.request
lessons.context: CORS is enforced by browsers, not servers. When tests run API calls through a browser page context, the browser enforces CORS and blocks cross-origin requests. APIRequestContext bypasses the browser entirely and is not subject to CORS restrictions.

problem.cause: API call made through browser page context — browser enforces CORS policy

antipattern.description: Using page.request for API calls that cross origins
antipattern.why_bad: Browser enforces CORS — cross-origin requests are blocked even if the server would allow them via direct HTTP
code: // BAD — uses browser context, subject to CORS

solution.description: Use playwright.request.newContext() which makes direct HTTP calls without browser CORS enforcement.

lessons.ai_instruction: For API tests, always use playwright.request.newContext() instead of page.request. This avoids CORS issues and is the correct approach for API-layer testing.
